provenance_defaults.source: self-authored (multi-agent authored + adversarially verified; general clinical pharmacology knowledge, AU/AMH context; corroborate against AMH/TGA at review). NOT derived from LITFL notes.
provenance_defaults.authored_by: claude-fable-5 workflow (author+verify agents; draft — KL to attest)

records.bioavailability: Approximately 60-80% oral; unaffected by food
records.metabolism: Hepatic via glucuronidation and CYP2C9 oxidation
records.elimination: Biliary/faecal and renal
records.notes: No pharmacologically significant active metabolites; suitable for once-daily dosing.

records.bioavailability: Near-complete oral absorption (~100%)
records.protein_binding: ~90%, saturable and decreasing at higher plasma concentrations
records.metabolism: Extensive hepatic metabolism via glucuronidation, mitochondrial beta-oxidation and some CYP oxidation
records.elimination: Renal, predominantly as metabolites
records.notes: Enzyme inhibitor; free fraction rises when binding sites saturate at high doses.

records.bioavailability: ~90% oral
records.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2C9 and CYP2C19; saturable (Michaelis-Menten) kinetics
records.elimination: Renal as inactive metabolites
records.notes: Narrow therapeutic index with non-linear (zero-order) kinetics at therapeutic doses; potent hepatic enzyme inducer; monitor plasma levels.

records.bioavailability: Low oral bioavailability due to extensive first-pass metabolism; given parenterally or topically
records.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP1A2 and CYP3A4
records.elimination: Renal, largely as metabolites
records.notes: Clearance is flow-limited and reduced in hepatic impairment or low cardiac output.

records.bioavailability: High, approximately 80-100%
records.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2C9
records.elimination: Renal as inactive metabolites

records.bioavailability: High, approximately 90-100%
records.metabolism: Limited hepatic metabolism
records.elimination: Renal, largely as unchanged drug
records.notes: Concentrates in urine, supporting use in urinary tract infection.

records.bioavailability: Negligible oral absorption; administered intravenously
records.metabolism: Minimal (small fraction metabolised hepatically to glycogen)
records.elimination: Renal via glomerular filtration, largely unchanged

records.bioavailability: Not orally active; given by IM, IV, subcutaneous or inhaled/nebulised routes
records.metabolism: Rapid metabolism by catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) and monoamine oxidase (MAO)
records.elimination: Renal as metabolites (metanephrine, vanillylmandelic acid)
records.notes: Extremely rapid onset and offset; not suitable for oral administration.

records.bioavailability: ~75% absorbed and converted to the active carboxylate
records.metabolism: Prodrug hydrolysed by hepatic esterases to active oseltamivir carboxylate; not significantly CYP-metabolised
records.elimination: Renal (active metabolite) via glomerular filtration and tubular secretion
records.notes: Dose reduction required in significant renal impairment.

records.bioavailability: Oral absorption incomplete and variable; base is acid-labile so salt/ester forms used
records.metabolism: Hepatic; a potent inhibitor of CYP3A4
records.elimination: Predominantly biliary excretion; minor renal
records.notes: CYP3A4 inhibition underlies numerous drug interactions; food can reduce absorption of some formulations

records.bioavailability: Both components well absorbed orally (approximately 90-100%)
records.metabolism: Hepatic (sulfamethoxazole acetylated and glucuronidated; trimethoprim oxidised)
records.elimination: Renal excretion of parent drug and metabolites
records.half_life: Both components approximately 8-11 hours (prolonged in renal impairment)
records.notes: Formulated in a 5:1 sulfamethoxazole-to-trimethoprim ratio; dose reduction required in renal impairment

records.bioavailability: Not orally absorbed; administered intravenously
records.metabolism: Partial hepatic deacetylation
records.elimination: Biliary/hepatic and renal
records.notes: Duration prolonged in hepatic or renal impairment and by accumulation of the active metabolite

records.bioavailability: Not orally active; given by intravenous infusion
records.metabolism: Rapidly metabolised by monoamine oxidase (MAO) and catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT)
records.elimination: Metabolites excreted renally
records.notes: Requires continuous infusion because of rapid clearance; titrated to haemodynamic response

records.bioavailability: Acid-labile with poor, unreliable oral absorption; given IV or IM
records.metabolism: Minimal
records.elimination: Predominantly renal via active tubular secretion
records.notes: Short half-life necessitates frequent dosing or continuous infusion; probenecid prolongs levels

records.bioavailability: Oral bioavailability approximately 77%
records.metabolism: Hepatic, primarily via CYP2C19 with a minor CYP3A4 contribution
records.elimination: Metabolites excreted mainly renally
records.half_life: Approximately 1 hour (acid suppression far outlasts plasma half-life due to irreversible pump binding)
records.notes: Enteric-coated to protect acid-labile drug; clinical effect prolonged despite short plasma half-life

records.bioavailability: Given intravenously, intramuscularly or subcutaneously; rapid onset after IV
records.metabolism: Not a substrate for COMT or MAO; taken up into adrenergic neurons
records.elimination: Renal excretion
records.notes: Longer duration of action than noradrenaline owing to resistance to COMT/MAO metabolism

records.bioavailability: Oral absorption of phytomenadione is bile-salt dependent and variable; parenteral routes used when rapid or reliable effect needed
records.metabolism: Hepatic
records.elimination: Metabolites excreted in bile and urine
records.notes: Onset of clotting-factor correction is delayed several hours (requires new factor synthesis); IV acts faster than oral

records.bioavailability: Oral bioavailability high (approximately 90%+); rapid absorption
records.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP2C19 and CYP3A4
records.active_metabolites: Desmethyldiazepam (nordazepam, long-acting), temazepam and oxazepam (all active)
records.elimination: Renal excretion of metabolites (largely as glucuronide conjugates)
records.half_life: Parent drug approximately 20-50 hours; active metabolite nordazepam up to ~100 hours
records.notes: Long effective half-life due to active metabolites causes accumulation with repeated dosing, especially in the elderly and hepatic impairment

records.bioavailability: Well absorbed orally (near-complete for immediate-release)
records.metabolism: Hepatic, principally CYP1A2 with CYP3A4 contribution
records.elimination: Hepatic metabolism with renal excretion of metabolites
records.half_life: ~8 h in healthy non-smoking adults (highly variable; shorter in smokers, longer in heart/liver failure)
records.notes: Narrow therapeutic index; requires plasma level monitoring and is subject to many drug and smoking interactions

records.bioavailability: Well absorbed orally
records.metabolism: Not metabolised
records.elimination: Renal excretion of unchanged drug
records.notes: Onset of diuresis diminishes with continued use as bicarbonate stores deplete

records.bioavailability: Oral ~14% (extensive first-pass); subcutaneous ~96%
records.metabolism: Hepatic, principally by monoamine oxidase-A
records.elimination: Renal excretion, largely as the inactive indole acetic acid metabolite
records.notes: Subcutaneous route gives rapid onset for severe attacks and cluster headache

records.bioavailability: Low and dose-dependent oral absorption (~10-20%)
records.metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism
records.elimination: Renal, by glomerular filtration and active tubular secretion
records.half_life: ~2.5-3 h with normal renal function (prolonged in renal impairment)
records.notes: Dose reduction required in renal impairment; adequate hydration reduces renal precipitation risk

records.metabolism: Extensive hepatic metabolism to inactive metabolites
records.elimination: Hepatic
records.notes: Half-life is shorter than most benzodiazepines, so resedation can occur and repeat dosing/observation may be needed

records.metabolism: Hepatic clearance (agent-dependent)
records.half_life: Very short circulating half-life (minutes); varies by agent (e.g. alteplase initial ~4-5 min, tenecteplase longer allowing bolus dosing)
records.notes: Class includes alteplase, tenecteplase, reteplase and streptokinase; administered intravenously; short half-life limits the fibrinolytic window but bleeding risk persists

records.bioavailability: Parenteral only; IM bioavailability ~100% (not given orally)
records.metabolism: Not appreciably metabolised
records.elimination: Dual elimination — renal (unchanged) and biliary
records.notes: Long half-life and dual excretion generally avoid the need for renal dose adjustment at usual doses

records.metabolism: Rapid cellular uptake and deamination by adenosine deaminase and phosphorylation
records.elimination: Metabolic (intracellular), independent of renal or hepatic function
records.notes: Must be given as a rapid IV bolus immediately followed by a saline flush; effect is essentially instantaneous and transient

records.bioavailability: Oral ~65-80% (variable first-pass metabolism)
records.metabolism: Hepatic
records.elimination: Renal excretion
records.notes: Dose reduction advised in renal and hepatic impairment and in the elderly

records.bioavailability: Well absorbed orally; reduced and delayed by food (best taken on an empty stomach)
records.metabolism: Hepatic deacetylation; potent inducer of CYP3A4 and other CYP enzymes, including autoinduction of its own metabolism
records.elimination: Predominantly biliary/faecal with some renal excretion
records.half_life: Approximately 3-4 hours, shortening with repeated dosing due to autoinduction
records.notes: Potent enzyme inducer causing many drug interactions; imparts harmless orange-red colour to urine, sweat and tears

records.bioavailability: Oral bioavailability approximately 70-80%
records.metabolism: Partial hepatic metabolism; inhibits CYP1A2
records.elimination: Mainly renal (a large fraction unchanged) with some biliary/faecal
records.notes: Absorption markedly reduced by divalent/trivalent cations - separate from antacids, dairy, iron, calcium and zinc

records.bioavailability: Well absorbed after oral administration
records.metabolism: Hepatic glucuronidation; inhibits CYP2C19 and CYP3A4
records.elimination: Renal, largely as inactive glucuronide metabolite
records.half_life: Approximately 1.5-4 hours; prolonged in hepatic impairment and neonates
records.notes: Impaired neonatal glucuronidation causes accumulation; systemic dosing requires care in liver impairment

records.metabolism: Hepatic; N-dealkylation and conjugation, primarily via CYP3A4 (to pipecolylxylidide)
records.elimination: Renal excretion of metabolites
records.half_life: Approximately 2.7 hours in adults; substantially prolonged in neonates
records.notes: Long-acting amide anaesthetic with intermediate onset; systemic absorption depends on dose, site and use of vasoconstrictor

records.bioavailability: Approximately 60-75%; reduced by food
records.metabolism: Partly metabolised to disulfide conjugates
records.elimination: Renal, a substantial fraction excreted unchanged
records.half_life: Approximately 2 hours (short; usually dosed 2-3 times daily)
records.notes: Best taken about 1 hour before meals; dose reduction needed in renal impairment

records.bioavailability: Oral cefuroxime axetil approximately 35-50%, improved when taken with food; parenteral cefuroxime fully available
records.metabolism: Axetil ester hydrolysed in the gut/mucosa to active cefuroxime; cefuroxime itself minimally metabolised
records.elimination: Renal, largely unchanged (glomerular filtration and tubular secretion)
records.active_metabolites: Cefuroxime (the active moiety released from the axetil prodrug)
records.notes: Dose adjustment required in significant renal impairment

records.bioavailability: Oral approximately 35-40% due to high first-pass metabolism; intramuscular approximately 90%
records.metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4 to 1-hydroxymidazolam
records.elimination: Renal, as glucuronide conjugates
records.notes: CYP3A4 substrate - clearance altered by inhibitors/inducers; accumulation possible with prolonged infusion or organ impairment

records.bioavailability: Not orally active (degraded in GI tract); given parenterally (IV/IM/SC) or intranasally
records.metabolism: Proteolytic degradation in liver, kidney and plasma
records.elimination: Hepatic and renal
records.notes: Rapid, short-lived effect; hyperglycaemic action depends on adequate hepatic glycogen stores

records.metabolism: Partial hepatic deacetylation
records.elimination: Predominantly renal, largely unchanged; markedly prolonged in renal impairment
records.active_metabolites: 3-desacetylpancuronium (3-hydroxy metabolite), with reduced neuromuscular blocking activity
records.notes: Vagolytic (can produce tachycardia); duration prolonged in renal and hepatic impairment

records.bioavailability: Well absorbed orally; absorption delayed by food
records.metabolism: Hepatic: alcohol dehydrogenase then aldehyde dehydrogenase; CYP2E1 at higher concentrations
records.elimination: Predominantly hepatic metabolism; small fraction excreted renally and via lungs
records.notes: Follows zero-order (saturable) kinetics at usual concentrations, so a fixed amount is cleared per unit time

records.bioavailability: Subcutaneous absorption slow and incomplete (roughly 20-40%)
records.half_life: IV approximately 4-9 hours; subcutaneous longer (~19-25 hours) owing to absorption-rate-limited kinetics
records.elimination: Cleared largely by receptor-mediated uptake; not renally excreted intact
records.notes: Onset of measurable erythropoietic response takes days; effect on haemoglobin is gradual over weeks

records.bioavailability: High oral bioavailability (~63-89%)
records.metabolism: Hepatic glucuronidation and sulfation; minor CYP2E1 oxidation to NAPQI which is normally detoxified by glutathione
records.elimination: Renal excretion of conjugated metabolites
records.active_metabolites: NAPQI (reactive, hepatotoxic when glutathione is depleted)

records.bioavailability: High (~90%) oral
records.metabolism: Hepatic, principally CYP2D6
records.elimination: Combined hepatic metabolism and renal excretion (~30% excreted unchanged renally)
records.notes: Clearance reduced in renal or hepatic impairment and by CYP2D6 poor-metaboliser status

records.metabolism: Rapidly hydrolysed in plasma by butyrylcholinesterase (plasma/pseudocholinesterase)
records.elimination: Plasma enzymatic hydrolysis
records.half_life: Very short; onset ~30-60 seconds, clinical duration ~5-10 minutes
records.notes: Action markedly prolonged in butyrylcholinesterase deficiency or with certain atypical genotypes

records.bioavailability: ~45% oral
records.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4; substrate of P-glycoprotein
records.elimination: Biliary/faecal excretion with a renal contribution; undergoes enterohepatic recirculation
records.notes: Narrow therapeutic index; clearance reduced by renal or hepatic impairment and by CYP3A4/P-glycoprotein inhibitors

records.bioavailability: Not absorbed orally; given IV or IM only
records.metabolism: Negligible
records.elimination: Renal, largely as unchanged drug

records.metabolism: Rapidly reduced in erythrocytes and tissues releasing cyanide, which is converted hepatically (with thiosulfate) to thiocyanate
records.elimination: Thiocyanate metabolite eliminated renally
records.half_life: Parent drug half-life is minutes; thiocyanate half-life ~3 days and longer in renal impairment
records.notes: Onset within seconds and offset within minutes, allowing minute-to-minute titration by infusion

records.bioavailability: Low and variable oral (~10-30%) due to extensive first-pass metabolism
records.metabolism: Extensive hepatic metabolism (including CYP2D6) to numerous metabolites
records.elimination: Excretion of metabolites in urine and bile

records.bioavailability: rapidly and essentially completely absorbed after ingestion
records.metabolism: hepatic oxidation by alcohol dehydrogenase to formaldehyde, then by aldehyde dehydrogenase to formic acid/formate; rate-limiting and saturable
records.elimination: predominantly via metabolism; small amounts excreted unchanged renally and via the lungs
records.half_life: variable and dose-dependent; elimination is saturable — untreated the apparent half-life is roughly on the order of 12-24 h, and it is markedly prolonged (reported ~45-50 h) once alcohol dehydrogenase is blocked by fomepizole or ethanol, when clearance shifts to slow renal/pulmonary routes
records.active_metabolites: formaldehyde and formic acid (formate) — the toxic species responsible for acidosis and visual injury
records.notes: Elimination follows zero-order (saturable) kinetics once metabolic capacity is exceeded.

records.volume_of_distribution: large, ~2.5-3 L/kg (the highest Vd among the amide local anaesthetics)
records.metabolism: hepatic (with additional renal and pulmonary) amide hydrolysis
records.active_metabolites: o-toluidine (a metabolite implicated in methaemoglobin formation)
records.notes: Rapid clearance and lower systemic toxicity than other amides; a component of EMLA (with lidocaine) for topical use.

records.metabolism: extensive hepatic oxidation; slow
records.elimination: hepatic metabolism with renal excretion of metabolites
records.half_life: long terminal elimination half-life (~11 h), but clinical effect is terminated much sooner
records.notes: Ultra-short clinical duration results from rapid redistribution of drug from brain to muscle and fat, not from elimination; accumulates and context-sensitive half-time lengthens with repeated doses or infusion.

Administered directly into the vascular compartment (100% 'bioavailable'). Distribution is determined by tonicity: isotonic crystalloids (e.g. 0.9% sodium chloride, compound sodium lactate) distribute through the extracellular fluid with roughly a quarter remaining intravascular, whereas 5% glucose distributes across total body water. Excess water and electrolytes are cleared predominantly by the kidneys.

records.bioavailability: high, ~90% orally
records.metabolism: hepatic; nitro-reduction (CYP3A4-mediated) to 7-amino-clonazepam followed by acetylation
records.elimination: renal excretion of metabolites (little unchanged drug)
records.active_metabolites: none clinically significant (the 7-amino metabolite is essentially inactive)

records.metabolism: not significantly metabolised
records.elimination: excreted largely unchanged in the urine
records.notes: Onset of diuresis within ~2 h; requires adequate renal function for efficacy and is relatively ineffective at low GFR.

Pharmacokinetics are incompletely characterised. It is a tertiary amine that crosses the blood-brain barrier, has a relatively long duration of action allowing once- or twice-daily dosing, undergoes hepatic metabolism, and has a slow onset when given orally (faster with parenteral use for acute dystonia).

records.volume_of_distribution: small, ~0.2-0.3 L/kg (confined largely to extracellular fluid)
records.metabolism: minimal metabolism; largely eliminated unchanged
records.elimination: predominantly hepatobiliary excretion with a smaller renal component
records.notes: Rapid onset (~60-90 s) makes it suitable for rapid-sequence intubation; its effect can be rapidly reversed by sugammadex (encapsulation) or, more slowly, by an anticholinesterase such as neostigmine.

records.bioavailability: ~60% orally (significant first-pass metabolism)
records.metabolism: extensive hepatic metabolism, mainly via CYP3A4 (also CYP1A2 and CYP2D6)
records.elimination: renal (metabolites) and faecal; <5% excreted unchanged
records.notes: Dose should be reduced in severe hepatic impairment (maximum 8 mg/day).

records.bioavailability: Very low oral bioavailability due to extensive first-pass metabolism; given IV, IM, SC or intranasally
records.metabolism: Hepatic, principally glucuronidation to naloxone-3-glucuronide
records.elimination: Renal excretion of metabolites
records.notes: Duration of action is often shorter than that of the opioid being reversed, so re-narcotisation can occur and repeat dosing may be needed

records.metabolism: Hepatic, by glucuronidation and CYP-mediated hydroxylation to inactive conjugates
records.elimination: Renal excretion of inactive metabolites
records.half_life: Rapid distribution half-life (2-8 minutes); context-sensitive with longer terminal elimination
records.notes: Rapid redistribution accounts for quick recovery after short procedures

records.bioavailability: High; the 15 mg and 20 mg doses should be taken with food to ensure near-complete absorption
records.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4, CYP2J2 and CYP-independent hydrolysis; sensitive to combined strong CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitors and inducers
records.elimination: About one third excreted unchanged by the kidneys, the remainder as metabolites (renal and faecal)
records.half_life: Approximately 5-9 hours in younger adults, 11-13 hours in the elderly
records.notes: No routine coagulation monitoring required

records.bioavailability: Oral absorption is partial (roughly a third) and vitamin D dependent
records.elimination: Renal excretion, with regulation by parathyroid hormone and vitamin D
records.notes: Distributes predominantly to bone; IV salts (gluconate/chloride) used when rapid correction is needed

records.bioavailability: High oral bioavailability (around 90% or more)
records.metabolism: Hepatic oxidation to an active hydroxy metabolite plus conjugation
records.elimination: Predominantly renal, with some faecal excretion
records.notes: Good tissue and CNS penetration

records.bioavailability: High (approximately 90-100%)
records.metabolism: Not appreciably metabolised
records.elimination: Excreted essentially unchanged by the kidneys
records.notes: Renal clearance mandates dose reduction and interval adjustment in renal impairment

records.bioavailability: Low and variable (around 25%) owing to extensive hepatic first-pass metabolism
records.metabolism: Hepatic, principally via CYP2D6 and CYP1A2
records.elimination: Hepatic metabolism with renal excretion of metabolites
records.notes: Lipophilic and crosses the blood-brain barrier

records.bioavailability: IV/IM only (not orally absorbed); IM absorption ~100%
records.metabolism: Minimal hepatic metabolism
records.elimination: Predominantly renal, ~85% excreted unchanged in urine
records.notes: Requires dose adjustment in renal impairment; accumulation risks neurotoxicity

records.bioavailability: ~37% oral
records.volume_of_distribution: Very large (~31 L/kg) reflecting extensive tissue uptake
records.metabolism: Limited hepatic demethylation
records.elimination: Mainly biliary/faecal excretion, minor renal
records.notes: Concentrates intracellularly and in tissue, allowing short courses and once-daily dosing

records.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 to active 10,11-epoxide; potent autoinducer of its own metabolism
records.elimination: Hepatic metabolism with renal excretion of metabolites
records.half_life: Initially 25-65 h, falling to ~12-17 h after autoinduction
records.notes: Autoinduction over 2-4 weeks necessitates dose review; strong enzyme inducer

records.bioavailability: Low when given alone due to peripheral decarboxylation; increased when combined with a decarboxylase inhibitor (carbidopa/benserazide)
records.metabolism: Extensive peripheral decarboxylation (DOPA decarboxylase) and O-methylation (COMT)
records.elimination: Renal excretion of metabolites
records.notes: Almost always co-formulated with a peripheral decarboxylase inhibitor; absorption reduced by high-protein meals

records.metabolism: Not significantly metabolised
records.elimination: Partly renal and substantially via intestinal/faecal route
records.notes: Does not accumulate significantly in renal impairment, so preferred tetracycline in renal disease; chelated by di/trivalent cations

records.bioavailability: ~72% oral
records.metabolism: Hepatic, chiefly CYP2D6, to active norfluoxetine
records.elimination: Renal excretion of metabolites
records.half_life: Fluoxetine 1-3 days (up to 4-6 days chronic); norfluoxetine 7-16 days
records.notes: Long half-life allows gradual washout; inhibits CYP2D6 causing interactions

records.bioavailability: Not orally absorbed; given IV or SC
records.metabolism: Cleared by reticuloendothelial system and hepatic depolymerisation
records.elimination: Saturable dose-dependent clearance
records.notes: Does not cross the placenta; effect monitored by aPTT and reversible with protamine

records.bioavailability: Low (~20-35%) due to extensive first-pass metabolism
records.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 to active norverapamil
records.elimination: Renal (metabolites) with minor faecal
records.half_life: ~3-7 h single dose, longer with chronic dosing
records.notes: First-pass metabolism gives higher IV potency; CYP3A4/P-gp substrate and inhibitor

records.bioavailability: Oral salts variably and often poorly absorbed; IV ~100%
records.elimination: Renal excretion
records.notes: Accumulates in renal impairment; serum levels, reflexes and respiration guide parenteral use

records.metabolism: Extensive hepatic metabolism via CYP450 (notably CYP2D6), with significant first-pass effect
records.active_metabolites: Yes - e.g. amitriptyline to nortriptyline, imipramine to desipramine (themselves active TCAs)
records.elimination: Renal excretion of hydroxylated and conjugated metabolites
records.half_life: Long and variable, commonly ~10-30 h (agent dependent), supporting once-daily dosing
records.notes: Narrow therapeutic index with dangerous overdose toxicity; CYP2D6 polymorphism affects clearance

records.bioavailability: High; well absorbed orally
records.metabolism: Not metabolised
records.elimination: Excreted essentially unchanged by the kidneys; handled like sodium (reabsorption increases with sodium depletion/dehydration)
records.half_life: Approximately 18-24 h; prolonged in the elderly and in renal impairment
records.notes: Narrow therapeutic index requiring serum level monitoring; clearance falls with sodium depletion, dehydration and NSAIDs/ACE inhibitors/thiazides

records.bioavailability: Poor oral (high first-pass); administered IV, transdermal or transmucosal (oral transmucosal ~50%)
records.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 to norfentanyl
records.active_metabolites: No clinically significant active metabolites (norfentanyl essentially inactive)
records.elimination: Renal excretion of metabolites
records.half_life: IV terminal ~2-4 h; context-sensitive half-time rises with prolonged infusion, and transdermal apparent half-life is longer due to depot absorption
records.notes: CYP3A4 interactions can markedly alter levels; lipophilic depot effect with patches/infusions

records.bioavailability: Near complete after subcutaneous injection
records.metabolism: Hepatic
records.elimination: Renal, with some unchanged drug in urine
records.half_life: Approximately 1.5-2 h after subcutaneous dosing (much longer than native somatostatin)
records.notes: Long-acting release (LAR) depot IM formulation allows dosing every ~4 weeks

records.bioavailability: IV 100%; oral low (~20-25%) due to extensive first-pass metabolism
records.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 and CYP2B6 (N-demethylation)
records.elimination: Renal excretion of metabolites
records.notes: Rapid CNS penetration and short redistribution phase give quick anaesthetic onset/offset

records.bioavailability: Not orally bioavailable (peptide degraded in gut); given subcutaneously, or IV in acute care
records.metabolism: Degraded by insulin-degrading enzyme in liver and kidney
records.elimination: Renal and hepatic clearance
records.half_life: Very short for endogenous/IV regular insulin (minutes); subcutaneous duration is determined by the formulation's absorption profile
records.notes: Onset/peak/duration are formulation dependent (rapid-acting, short, intermediate, long-acting analogues); clearance reduced in renal impairment

records.bioavailability: Well absorbed orally
records.metabolism: Extensive hepatic metabolism (CYP-mediated) to essentially inactive metabolites
records.elimination: Renal excretion of metabolites; little unchanged drug
records.half_life: Approximately 10-12 h (immediate release); modified-release formulation gives a longer effective profile allowing once-daily dosing
records.notes: Metabolised to inactive products, so lower accumulation risk in renal impairment than renally-cleared sulfonylureas

records.bioavailability: Well absorbed; crosses the blood-brain and placental barriers
records.metabolism: Partial hepatic metabolism
records.elimination: Renal; roughly half excreted unchanged in urine
records.half_life: Approximately 2-4 h in adults (longer in young children and the elderly)

records.bioavailability: Relatively high oral bioavailability, ~60-87%
records.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 (to noroxycodone) and CYP2D6 (to oxymorphone)
records.active_metabolites: Oxymorphone (active) and noroxycodone; parent drug is the main contributor to effect
records.elimination: Renal excretion of drug and metabolites
records.half_life: Approximately 3-4.5 h (immediate release); controlled-release formulations extend the apparent duration
records.notes: CYP2D6 and CYP3A4 activity/interactions influence metabolite exposure and response

records.metabolism: Hepatic (predominantly CYP3A4 for many synthetic agents)
records.elimination: Renal excretion of inactive metabolites
records.notes: Pharmacokinetics vary markedly by agent: plasma half-lives are short (e.g. hydrocortisone ~1.5 h, dexamethasone ~3-4 h) but biological (anti-inflammatory) effect far outlasts plasma levels; prednisolone is the active moiety of prednisone. Class-level entry only.

records.bioavailability: Very low orally (<1%) due to extensive first-pass metabolism; given sublingual, transdermal, IV or spray
records.metabolism: Rapid hepatic and vascular denitration (glutathione-S-transferase/organic nitrate reductase)
records.elimination: Renal excretion of metabolites

records.bioavailability: Well absorbed; ~60% reaching systemic circulation after first-pass; absorption not affected by food
records.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP1A2 (minor CYP2D6) and direct glucuronidation
records.half_life: Approximately 30-38 hours (longer in elderly/women, shorter in smokers)
records.elimination: Mainly renal excretion of metabolites
records.notes: Cigarette smoking induces CYP1A2 and lowers plasma concentrations; dose review needed on stopping/starting smoking.

records.bioavailability: Approximately 50-60%
records.metabolism: Extensive hepatic metabolism (demethylation and conjugation)
records.elimination: Predominantly biliary/faecal excretion

records.bioavailability: Negligible oral absorption (oral route used only for gut infection such as C. difficile); IV for systemic infection
records.half_life: Approximately 4-6 hours with normal renal function; markedly prolonged in renal impairment
records.elimination: Renal, largely unchanged via glomerular filtration
records.notes: Requires therapeutic drug monitoring (trough or AUC-guided dosing); clearance falls with declining renal function.

records.metabolism: Hepatic prodrug activation, chiefly CYP2C19 (also CYP3A4/2B6) via a two-step oxidation to an active thiol metabolite; most drug is hydrolysed by esterases to an inactive acid
records.half_life: Parent/active metabolite short (~6 h), but antiplatelet effect persists for platelet lifespan (~7-10 days) due to irreversible binding
records.elimination: Roughly equal renal and faecal excretion
records.notes: CYP2C19 poor metabolisers have reduced active-metabolite formation and attenuated antiplatelet response.

records.bioavailability: Low oral bioavailability (~6-10%) due to first-pass metabolism; IV used for paracetamol poisoning
records.metabolism: Hepatic; incorporated into endogenous sulfur/cysteine and glutathione pools
records.elimination: Renal excretion

records.bioavailability: Oral approximately 60-70%
records.metabolism: Hepatic via CYP3A4 and CYP2D6, and glucuronidation (including reversible reduction to reduced haloperidol)
records.half_life: Approximately 14-26 hours (oral); longer for depot decanoate formulation
records.elimination: Renal and biliary/faecal excretion

records.bioavailability: Ramipril well absorbed; active ramiprilat bioavailability ~28-45%
records.metabolism: Hepatic hydrolysis (esterase) of the prodrug to active ramiprilat
records.half_life: Effective/terminal half-life of ramiprilat is long (~13-17 h), supporting once-daily dosing
records.elimination: Predominantly renal; dose reduction needed in renal impairment
